summary: Julia Evans shares her excitement about PaintsChainer, a machine learning tool that automatically colors black and white images. She experiments with it on her zine artwork and traces photos, exploring how ML art tools can augment creative ability. The post reflects on what makes certain AI art tools compelling versus others, concluding that the best tools are ones where the output still feels like something you personally made. She contrasts PaintsChainer favorably with deepart.io, noting that creative control over the process matters more than raw impressiveness.
key_points: PaintsChainer uses machine learning to automatically color black and white drawings, producing surprisingly compelling results
The most exciting ML art tools are ones where the output feels like something you made yourself, not just algorithmic transformation
Combining multiple accessible tools (tablet tracing + ML coloring) can produce results far beyond one's unaided skill level
Deepart.io's style transfer is impressive but less personally satisfying because you have less creative control over the result
Neural network applications that augment human creativity are more exciting than ones that replace it
money_quotes: I want to use magical art tools to make things that feel like I made them.
I find this really cool because I can make an image with my hands with a lot less skills than I would have needed without this magical technology.
It feel more like a thing that I made because I have a lot of creative control over what it looks like.
I spent a bunch of time playing with deepart.io but couldn't make anything I really was excited about.
This kind of thing is probably the application of neural networks that makes me the most excited.
key_insight: The most compelling ML art tools are ones that augment your creative process while preserving your sense of authorship, letting you make things that still feel like yours.
